_comment: Drone LiDAR ingest — LAS / LAZ file from a UAV-mounted laser
scanner into a MobilityDB tpcpatch column.
Prerequisites:
1. Register a pcid that matches the drone's dim layout. See
examples/drone_lidar_ingest.sql for the typical XYZ + I + GPS
time + Classification schema used here (pcid 500).
2. Have a target table:
CREATE TABLE drone_traj (id serial PRIMARY KEY,
traj tpcpatch);
What this pipeline does:
- readers.las opens the LAS / LAZ file and streams points.
- filters.assign derives time_t (Unsigned64 microseconds since
Unix epoch) from the LAS GpsTime field. The expression below
assumes GpsTime is already in seconds-since-Unix-epoch (the
convention emitted by most modern UAV processing toolchains).
For LAS files using 'Adjusted GPS Standard Time' (header
global_encoding bit 0 set) you need to add 315964782 seconds
to bridge the GPS-Unix epoch difference, e.g.
where 18 is the 2024 GPS-UTC leap-second offset.
- filters.sort orders by time_t so the writer groups consecutive
same-timestamp points into one pcpatch.
- writers.tpcpatch INSERTs one tpcpatch row whose instants are
the per-timestamp pcpatches. Set 'compression':'dimensional'
on writers.tpcpatch to shrink the SQL INSERT payload by ~3x
on dim-redundant data (the on-disk tpcpatch is normalised to
PC_NONE either way).
For a real drone LiDAR file with microsecond-precision GpsTime,
every point typically has a unique timestamp and the result is a
1-point-per-instant tpcpatch. To group neighbouring points into
richer instants, insert a filters.assign that quantises time_t
to a coarser grid (e.g. 100 ms buckets) before the sort.
